1.note: I sit as a queen, I am no widow, and mourning I shall never see. For this reason her plagues will come in a single day, death and mourning and famine — Revelation's Babylon is constructed as the anti-Jerusalem: where Jerusalem lamented that she had fallen from greatness to widowhood (Lam 1:1: how lonely sits the city that was full of people; she has become like a widow), Babylon boasts she will never become a widow. The irony of Revelation 18 depends on the reader knowing Lamentations: Babylon's boast is precisely what Jerusalem had before the fall; Babylon's judgment is precisely what Jerusalem suffered.

12.note: Those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads — Lamentations 1:12 (is it nothing to you, all who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow) describes Jerusalem's suffering from passers-by who do not stop. The Good Friday tradition identifies this verse with Christ's passion, and the Gospel accounts activate the connection: both Matthew (27:39) and Mark (15:29) note that those who passed by (hoi paraporeuomenoi) mocked Jesus. The crucified one takes up Jerusalem's voice of forsaken suffering.

14.note: They say continually to those who despise the word of the LORD: it shall be well with you — Lamentations 2:14's accusation that Jerusalem's prophets gave false and deceptive visions and did not expose iniquity to restore her fortunes is the direct result of the false-prophecy that Jeremiah had diagnosed for decades before the fall. Jer 23:16-17 is the prophetic critique; Lam 2:14 is the post-fall verdict: those false visions led directly to this catastrophe. Both texts are almost certainly responding to the same prophetic crisis.

22.note: If we are faithless, he remains faithful — for he cannot deny himself — Paul's assurance that God's faithfulness does not depend on human faithfulness is the NT crystallization of the theological insight that Lamentations 3:22-23 articulates in the depths of national catastrophe. The most astonishing claim of Lamentations is that YHWH's steadfast love has not ceased even when Jerusalem lies in ruins; 2 Tim 2:13 extends that same claim to the individual in failure. Faithfulness is not a response to our merit but an expression of who YHWH is.

The steadfast love of the LORD never ceases; his mercies never come to an end — the great confession of YHWH's faithfulness in the midst of Jerusalem's total destruction; Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever (Heb 13:8) is the new covenant form of the same confession: divine steadfast love, permanent and unending
It is good that one should wait quietly for the salvation of the LORD — the sufferer's renewed hope after total collapse; the salvation (yeshuah) that Lamentations awaits is the name of Jesus

27.note: For the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it — Lamentations 3:26-27 counsels sitting silently and bearing the yoke in youth as what is good when under YHWH's discipline. Hebrews 12's theology of divine discipline as training draws on this same tradition: present suffering under the yoke is not abandonment but formation; the fruit of righteousness comes later.

55.note: Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD! Lord, hear my voice! — the de profundis psalm opens with the identical idiom as Lam 3:55: I called on your name, O LORD, from the depths of the pit. Both texts move from the cry in the depths to the confidence that YHWH has heard; both make the transition from cry to hope based solely on YHWH's steadfast love (hesed) and his character as one who hears the desperate. The psalm and Lamentations draw on the same theological grammar of prayer-from-the-abyss.

20.note: The kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers were gathered together, against the Lord and against his anointed — Luke quotes Ps 2:1-2 in Acts 4:25-26 to interpret the arrest and crucifixion of Jesus. Lam 4:20 is the historical instance of the LORD's anointed being taken in the pits — the Davidic king captured, the covenant seeming to fail, the breath of our nostrils gone. The NT anointed (christos) walks the same road as the Davidic king of Lam 4:20, but the NT story adds what Lamentations could not yet say: the anointed who was captured is raised and vindicated.

1.note: 'Remember, O LORD, what has befallen us; look, and see our disgrace!' The exiles' plea for divine attention to their suffering anticipates the cry of the martyrs under the altar: 'O Sovereign Lord, holy and true, how long before you will judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth?' (Rev 6:10). Both are cries from the depths of visible defeat, addressed to a God who appears not to see — and both trust that he does, and will act.

7.note: 'Our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities.' The problem of generational guilt — suffering that exceeds the individual's own sin — is the question the disciples raise in John 9:2: 'who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?' Jesus's answer reframes the question entirely (John 9:3), but the Lamentations confession shows the lived experience behind the disciples' theology: the people of Judah were bearing exile as the consequence of accumulated ancestral sin.

16.note: 'The crown has fallen from our head; woe to us, for we have sinned!' Jerusalem's lost crown — the royal dignity and covenant privilege stripped by exile — is the OT image behind Christ's warning: 'hold what you have, so that no one may seize your crown' (Rev 3:11). The Lamentations community has already experienced the crown's loss; Christ warns the church in Philadelphia that what they have can likewise be lost through compromise.

19.note: 'But you, O LORD, reign forever; your throne endures to all generations.' In the middle of utter desolation — temple burned, city ruined, people scattered — the poet affirms divine sovereignty. The author of Hebrews applies the same 'forever throne' language to the Son, citing Psalm 45:6: 'your throne, O God, is forever and ever.' The permanence that Lamentations finds in YHWH despite catastrophe is given its final referent in the Son who is the same yesterday, today, and forever.

The community's anchor in exile is 'your throne endures to all generations' — the throne remains even when Jerusalem's throne has fallen. John's inaugural vision shows the throne that never falls: 'behold, a throne stood in heaven, with one seated on the throne' (Rev 4:2). All earthly thrones collapse; the heavenly throne is the constant that Lamentations clings to from below.

21.note: 'Restore us to yourself, O LORD, that we may be restored! Renew our days as of old.' The prayer for restoration is the OT form of Peter's Pentecost call: 'Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out, that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord' (Acts 3:19). The exilic restoration prayer is answered — partially in the return from Babylon, fully in the new covenant — by the God who restores and refreshes.

The prayer for renewal — 'renew our days as of old' — seeks the restoration of what was lost. John's Apocalypse shows the ultimate answer: 'Behold, I am making all things new' (Rev 21:5). The exile community asks for days renewed; the enthroned Christ promises creation renewed. The scope expands from a nation's restoration to a cosmos renewed.

22.note: The book ends on an almost unbearable question: 'Or have you utterly rejected us? Are you exceedingly angry with us?' — the fear that the covenant is finally and permanently broken, that God is done with Israel. Paul answers from the resurrection-side of history: 'I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means!' (Rom 11:1). The existential dread of Lamentations 5:22 is the question that Romans 11 explicitly addresses: divine covenant faithfulness survives even the catastrophe of Israel's exile and the nation's rejection of its Messiah.
